mappings.rationale: The rise of Russia under Ivan III, Ivan IV, Peter the Great, and Catherine the Great, including expansion, westernization, and the Time of Troubles, are central to this section.
Habsburg rivalry with the Ottomans, Austrian conquest of Hungary, and the Austro-Turkish wars are major themes of this section.
The Thirty Years' War's impact on eastern European states and the Habsburg-Ottoman rivalry intersect with Holy Roman Empire history.
The Ottoman Empire's Islamic character, Safavid Shi'ite Iran, and Mughal India's Islamic governance are key to understanding the region.
The Ottoman, Safavid, and Mughal empires represent the major Islamic powers of the early modern period covered in this section.
The Safavid dynasty, conflict with the Turks, Abbas I's restoration of the state, and the rise of Zand and Qajar dynasties are explicitly covered.
The Ottoman, Russian, Safavid, and Mughal empires, and their expansions and conflicts, are major imperial systems covered in this section.
The Mughal Empire's decline and the rise of British power in India, including the Battle of Plassey and consolidation under Hastings, are covered.
The Hindu Maratha confederacy's challenge to Mughal power and Hindu-Muslim relations in India are relevant to this section.
The Sikhs' military challenge to the Mughals and the decline of Mughal authority in northwestern India are relevant to this section's coverage of Indian history.
Ottoman-European diplomacy, Russian-Turkish relations, and the complex alliances among Eastern European powers involve diplomatic history.
The emergence of national consciousness among Poles, Hungarians, and the various peoples of the Ottoman and Russian empires connects to early nationalism.
The Ottoman conquest of Constantinople (1453) and the end of the Byzantine Empire provide the starting point for this section's coverage of the Ottoman state.
The intellectual life of the Ottoman, Safavid, and Mughal empires involved continuing traditions of Islamic philosophy.
The establishment of British power in India through the East India Company, the Battle of Plassey, and consolidation under Hastings are covered.
The Ottoman-Habsburg rivalry, Russian expansion, and the contest for control of trade routes and strategic territories are geopolitical themes.
The development of the Polish Diet, Hungarian political institutions, and Russian institutional developments connect to the broader history of representative governance.
The Tokugawa period in Japan, mentioned as context for the Meiji era in the adjacent section, connects to the warrior traditions discussed here.
The three great Islamic empires of the early modern period -- Ottoman, Safavid, and Mughal -- represent Islam's global political and cultural reach.
Ottoman and Safavid control of trade routes connecting East and West, and the overland trade networks across Central Asia, connect to the Silk Road's legacy.
